question:
Prove that there is a constant $C$ such that, if $p(x)$ is a polynomial
of degree 1999, then
\[|p(0)|\leq C \int_{-1}^1 |p(x)|\,dx.\]
solution:
First solution: (by Reid Barton)
Let $r_1, \dots, r_{1999}$ be the roots of $P$. Draw a disc of radius
$\epsilon$ around each $r_i$, where $\epsilon < 1/3998$; this
disc covers a subinterval of $[-1/2,1/2]$
of length at most $2\epsilon$, and so of the 2000 (or fewer) uncovered
intervals in $[-1/2,1/2]$,
one, which we call $I$, has length at least $\delta = (1-3998\epsilon)/2000
> 0$.
We will exhibit an explicit lower bound for the integral of $|P(x)|/P(0)$ over this
interval, which will yield such a bound for the entire integral.

Note that
\[
\frac{|P(x)|}{|P(0)|} = \prod_{i=1}^{1999} \frac{|x-r_i|}{|r_i|}.
\]
Also note that by construction, $|x - r_i| \geq \epsilon$ for each $x \in I$.
If $|r_i| \leq 1$, then we have $\frac{|x-r_i|}{|r_i|} \geq \epsilon$. If $|r_i| > 1$, then
\[
\frac{|x-r_i|}{|r_i|} = |1 - x/r_i| \geq 1 - |x/r_i| \geq = 1/2
> \epsilon.
\]
We conclude that $\int_I |P(x)/P(0)|\,dx \geq \delta \epsilon$,
independent of $P$.

Second solution:
It will be a bit more convenient to assume $P(0) = 1$ (which we
may achieve by rescaling unless $P(0)=0$, in which case
there is nothing to prove) and to prove that there exists $D>0$
such that $\int_{-1}^1 |P(x)|\,dx \geq D$, or even
such that $\int_0^1 |P(x)|\,dx \geq D$.

We first reduce to the case where $P$ has all of its roots in
$[0,1]$. If this is not the case, we can
factor $P(x)$ as $Q(x) R(x)$, where $Q$ has all roots in
the interval and $R$ has none. Then $R$ is either always
positive or always negative on $[0,1]$; assume the former.
Let $k$ be the largest positive real number such that
$R(x) - kx \geq 0$ on $[0,1]$; then
\begin{align*}
\int_{-1}^1 |P(x)|\,dx &= \int_{-1}^1 |Q(x)R(x)|\,dx \\
&> \int_{-1}^1 |Q(x)(R(x)-kx)|\,dx,
\end{align*}
and $Q(x)(R(x)-kx)$ has more roots in $[0,1]$ than does $P$
(and has the same value at 0).
Repeating this argument shows that $\int_{0}^1 |P(x)|\,dx$
is greater than the corresponding integral for some polynomial
with all of its roots in $[0,1]$.

Under this assumption, we have
\[
P(x) = c \prod_{i=1}^{1999} (x-r_i)
\]
for some $r_i \in (0,1]$. Since
\[
P(0) = -c \prod r_i = 1,
\]
we have
\[
|c| \geq \prod |r_i^{-1}| \geq 1.
\]

Thus it suffices to prove that if $Q(x)$ is a \emph{monic} polynomial
of degree 1999 with all of its roots in $[0,1]$,
then $\int_0^1 |Q(x)|\,dx \geq D$ for some constant $D>0$. But the
integral of $\int_0^1 \prod_{i=1}^{1999} |x-r_i|\,dx$ is a continuous
function for $r_i \in [0,1]$. The product of all of these intervals is
compact, so the integral achieves a minimum value for some $r_i$. This
minimum is the desired $D$.

Third solution (by Abe Kunin):
It suffices to prove the stronger inequality
\[
\sup_{x \in [-1,1]} |P(x)| \leq C \int_{-1}^1 |P(x)|\,dx
\]
holds for some $C$.
But this follows immediately from the following standard fact: any two
norms on a finite-dimensional vector space (here the polynomials of
degree at most 1999) are equivalent. (The proof of this statement is also
a compactness argument: $C$ can be taken to be the maximum of the
L1-norm divided by the sup norm over the set of polynomials with
L1-norm 1.)

Note: combining the first two approaches gives a constructive solution with
a constant that is better than that given by the first solution,
but is still far from optimal. I don't know
offhand whether it is even known what the optimal constant and/or the
polynomials achieving that constant are.

descriptive_long.question:
Prove that there is a constant $constcap$ such that, if $polyfunc(axisvar)$ is a polynomial
of degree 1999, then
\[|polyfunc(0)|\leq constcap \int_{-1}^1 |polyfunc(axisvar)|\,daxisvar.\]
descriptive_long.solution:
First solution: (by Reid Barton)
Let $rootitem_1, \dots, rootitem_{1999}$ be the roots of $polymain$. Draw a disc of radius
$varepsil$ around each $rootitem_i$, where $varepsil < 1/3998$; this
disc covers a subinterval of $[-1/2,1/2]$
of length at most $2varepsil$, and so of the 2000 (or fewer) uncovered
intervals in $[-1/2,1/2]$,
one, which we call $I$, has length at least $vardelta = (1-3998varepsil)/2000
> 0$.
We will exhibit an explicit lower bound for the integral of $|polymain(axisvar)|/polymain(0)$ over this
interval, which will yield such a bound for the entire integral.

Note that
\[
\frac{|polymain(axisvar)|}{|polymain(0)|} = \prod_{i=1}^{1999} \frac{|axisvar-rootitem|}{|rootitem|}.
\]
Also note that by construction, $|axisvar - rootitem| \geq varepsil$ for each $axisvar \in I$.
If $|rootitem| \leq 1$, then we have $\frac{|axisvar-rootitem|}{|rootitem|} \geq varepsil$. If $|rootitem| > 1$, then
\[
\frac{|axisvar-rootitem|}{|rootitem|} = |1 - axisvar/rootitem| \geq 1 - |axisvar/rootitem| \geq = 1/2
> varepsil.
\]
We conclude that $\int_I |polymain(axisvar)/polymain(0)|\,daxisvar \geq vardelta varepsil$,
independent of $polymain$.

Second solution:
It will be a bit more convenient to assume $polymain(0) = 1$ (which we
may achieve by rescaling unless $polymain(0)=0$, in which case
there is nothing to prove) and to prove that there exists $constdee>0$
such that $\int_{-1}^1 |polymain(axisvar)|\,daxisvar \geq constdee$, or even
such that $\int_0^1 |polymain(axisvar)|\,daxisvar \geq constdee$.

We first reduce to the case where $polymain$ has all of its roots in
$[0,1]$. If this is not the case, we can
factor $polymain(axisvar)$ as $factorone(axisvar) factortwo(axisvar)$, where $factorone$ has all roots in
the interval and $factortwo$ has none. Then $factortwo$ is either always
positive or always negative on $[0,1]$; assume the former.
Let $scalefac$ be the largest positive real number such that
$factortwo(axisvar) - scalefac axisvar \geq 0$ on $[0,1]$; then
\begin{align*}
\int_{-1}^1 |polymain(axisvar)|\,daxisvar &= \int_{-1}^1 |factorone(axisvar)factortwo(axisvar)|\,daxisvar \\
&> \int_{-1}^1 |factorone(axisvar)(factortwo(axisvar)-scalefac axisvar)|\,daxisvar,
\end{align*}
and $factorone(axisvar)(factortwo(axisvar)-scalefac axisvar)$ has more roots in $[0,1]$ than does $polymain$
(and has the same value at 0).
Repeating this argument shows that $\int_{0}^1 |polymain(axisvar)|\,daxisvar$
is greater than the corresponding integral for some polynomial
with all of its roots in $[0,1]$.

Under this assumption, we have
\[
polymain(axisvar) = leadcoef \prod_{i=1}^{1999} (axisvar-rootitem)
\]
for some $rootitem \in (0,1]$. Since
\[
polymain(0) = -leadcoef \prod rootitem = 1,
\]
we have
\[
|leadcoef| \geq \prod |rootitem^{-1}| \geq 1.
\]

Thus it suffices to prove that if $factorone(axisvar)$ is a \emph{monic} polynomial
of degree 1999 with all of its roots in $[0,1]$,
then $\int_0^1 |factorone(axisvar)|\,daxisvar \geq constdee$ for some constant $constdee>0$. But the
integral of $\int_0^1 \prod_{i=1}^{1999} |axisvar-rootitem|\,daxisvar$ is a continuous
function for $rootitem \in [0,1]$. The product of all of these intervals is
compact, so the integral achieves a minimum value for some $rootitem$. This
minimum is the desired $constdee$.

Third solution (by Abe Kunin):
It suffices to prove the stronger inequality
\[
\sup_{axisvar \in [-1,1]} |polymain(axisvar)| \leq constcap \int_{-1}^1 |polymain(axisvar)|\,daxisvar
\]
holds for some $constcap$.
But this follows immediately from the following standard fact: any two
norms on a finite-dimensional vector space (here the polynomials of
degree at most 1999) are equivalent. (The proof of this statement is also
a compactness argument: $constcap$ can be taken to be the maximum of the
L1-norm divided by the sup norm over the set of polynomials with
L1-norm 1.)

Note: combining the first two approaches gives a constructive solution with
a constant that is better than that given by the first solution,
but is still far from optimal. I don't know
offhand whether it is even known what the optimal constant and/or the
polynomials achieving that constant are.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Prove that there is a constant $whirlwind$ such that, if $lanternfish(orchardview)$ is a polynomial
of degree 1999, then
\[|lanternfish(0)|\leq whirlwind \int_{-1}^1 |lanternfish(orchardview)|\,dorchardview.\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First solution: (by Reid Barton)
Let $r_1, \dots, r_{1999}$ be the roots of $moonlitsea$. Draw a disc of radius
$\dragonfly$ around each $buttercup$, where $\dragonfly < 1/3998$; this
disc covers a subinterval of $[-1/2,1/2]$
of length at most $2\dragonfly$, and so of the 2000 (or fewer) uncovered
intervals in $[-1/2,1/2]$,
one, which we call $I$, has length at least $\silhouette = (1-3998\dragonfly)/2000
> 0$.
We will exhibit an explicit lower bound for the integral of $|moonlitsea(orchardview)|/moonlitsea(0)$ over this
interval, which will yield such a bound for the entire integral.

Note that
\[
\frac{|moonlitsea(orchardview)|}{|moonlitsea(0)|} = \prod_{i=1}^{1999} \frac{|orchardview-buttercup|}{|buttercup|}.
\]
Also note that by construction, $|orchardview - buttercup| \geq \dragonfly$ for each $orchardview \in I$.
If $|buttercup| \leq 1$, then we have $\frac{|orchardview-buttercup|}{|buttercup|} \geq \dragonfly$. If $|buttercup| > 1$, then
\[
\frac{|orchardview-buttercup|}{|buttercup|} = |1 - orchardview/buttercup| \geq 1 - |orchardview/buttercup| \geq = 1/2
> \dragonfly.
\]
We conclude that $\int_I |moonlitsea(orchardview)/moonlitsea(0)|\,dorchardview \geq \silhouette \dragonfly$,
independent of $moonlitsea$.

Second solution:
It will be a bit more convenient to assume $moonlitsea(0) = 1$ (which we
may achieve by rescaling unless $moonlitsea(0)=0$, in which case
there is nothing to prove) and to prove that there exists $plaintiff>0$
such that $\int_{-1}^1 |moonlitsea(orchardview)|\,dorchardview \geq plaintiff$, or even
such that $\int_0^1 |moonlitsea(orchardview)|\,dorchardview \geq plaintiff$.

We first reduce to the case where $moonlitsea$ has all of its roots in
$[0,1]$. If this is not the case, we can
factor $moonlitsea(orchardview)$ as $cobblestone(orchardview) glimmerpath(orchardview)$, where $cobblestone$ has all roots in
the interval and $glimmerpath$ has none. Then $glimmerpath$ is either always
positive or always negative on $[0,1]$; assume the former.
Let $tangerine$ be the largest positive real number such that
$glimmerpath(orchardview) - tangerine orchardview \geq 0$ on $[0,1]$; then
\begin{align*}
\int_{-1}^1 |moonlitsea(orchardview)|\,dorchardview &= \int_{-1}^1 |cobblestone(orchardview)glimmerpath(orchardview)|\,dorchardview \\
&> \int_{-1}^1 |cobblestone(orchardview)(glimmerpath(orchardview)-tangerine orchardview)|\,dorchardview,
\end{align*}
and $cobblestone(orchardview)(glimmerpath(orchardview)-tangerine orchardview)$ has more roots in $[0,1]$ than does $moonlitsea$
(and has the same value at 0).
Repeating this argument shows that $\int_{0}^1 |moonlitsea(orchardview)|\,dorchardview$
is greater than the corresponding integral for some polynomial
with all of its roots in $[0,1]$.

Under this assumption, we have
\[
moonlitsea(orchardview) = honeybadger \prod_{i=1}^{1999} (orchardview-buttercup)
\]
for some $buttercup \in (0,1]$. Since
\[
moonlitsea(0) = -honeybadger \prod buttercup = 1,
\]
we have
\[
|honeybadger| \geq \prod |buttercup^{-1}| \geq 1.
\]

Thus it suffices to prove that if $cobblestone(orchardview)$ is a \emph{monic} polynomial
of degree 1999 with all of its roots in $[0,1]$,
then $\int_0^1 |cobblestone(orchardview)|\,dorchardview \geq plaintiff$ for some constant $plaintiff>0$. But the
integral of $\int_0^1 \prod_{i=1}^{1999} |orchardview-buttercup|\,dorchardview$ is a continuous
function for $buttercup \in [0,1]$. The product of all of these intervals is
compact, so the integral achieves a minimum value for some $buttercup$. This
minimum is the desired $plaintiff$.

Third solution (by Abe Kunin):
It suffices to prove the stronger inequality
\[
\sup_{orchardview \in [-1,1]} |moonlitsea(orchardview)| \leq whirlwind \int_{-1}^1 |moonlitsea(orchardview)|\,dorchardview
\]
holds for some $whirlwind$.
But this follows immediately from the following standard fact: any two
norms on a finite-dimensional vector space (here the polynomials of
degree at most 1999) are equivalent. (The proof of this statement is also
a compactness argument: $whirlwind$ can be taken to be the maximum of the
L1-norm divided by the sup norm over the set of polynomials with
L1-norm 1.)

Note: combining the first two approaches gives a constructive solution with
a constant that is better than that given by the first solution,
but is still far from optimal. I don't know
offhand whether it is even known what the optimal constant and/or the
polynomials achieving that constant are.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Prove that there is a constant $unbounded$ such that, if $nonpolynom(constantvalue)$ is a polynomial
of degree 1999, then
\[|nonpolynom(0)|\leq unbounded \int_{-1}^1 |nonpolynom(constantvalue)|\,dconstantvalue.\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First solution: (by Reid Barton)
Let $r_1, \dots, r_{1999}$ be the roots of $nonfunction$. Draw a disc of radius
$\largeness$ around each $peakvalue$, where $\largeness < 1/3998$; this
disc covers a subinterval of $[-1/2,1/2]$
of length at most $2\largeness$, and so of the 2000 (or fewer) uncovered
intervals in $[-1/2,1/2]$,
one, which we call $I$, has length at least $\vastness = (1-3998\largeness)/2000
> 0$.
We will exhibit an explicit lower bound for the integral of $|nonfunction(constantvalue)|/nonfunction(0)$ over this
interval, which will yield such a bound for the entire integral.

Note that
\[
\frac{|nonfunction(constantvalue)|}{|nonfunction(0)|} = \prod_{i=1}^{1999} \frac{|constantvalue-peakvalue|}{|peakvalue|}.
\]
Also note that by construction, $|constantvalue - peakvalue| \geq \largeness$ for each $constantvalue \in I$.
If $|peakvalue| \leq 1$, then we have $\frac{|constantvalue-peakvalue|}{|peakvalue|} \geq \largeness$. If $|peakvalue| > 1$, then
\[
\frac{|constantvalue-peakvalue|}{|peakvalue|} = |1 - constantvalue/peakvalue| \geq 1 - |constantvalue/peakvalue| \geq = 1/2
> \largeness.
\]
We conclude that $\int_I |nonfunction(constantvalue)/nonfunction(0)|\,dconstantvalue \geq \vastness \largeness$,
independent of $nonfunction$.

Second solution:
It will be a bit more convenient to assume $nonfunction(0) = 1$ (which we
may achieve by rescaling unless $nonfunction(0)=0$, in which case
there is nothing to prove) and to prove that there exists $smallvalue>0$
such that $\int_{-1}^1 |nonfunction(constantvalue)|\,dconstantvalue \geq smallvalue$, or even
such that $\int_0^1 |nonfunction(constantvalue)|\,dconstantvalue \geq smallvalue$.

We first reduce to the case where $nonfunction$ has all of its roots in
$[0,1]$. If this is not the case, we can
factor $nonfunction(constantvalue)$ as $irrelevant(constantvalue) inclusive(constantvalue)$, where $irrelevant$ has all roots in
the interval and $inclusive$ has none. Then $inclusive(constantvalue)$ is either always
positive or always negative on $[0,1]$; assume the former.
Let $minimalvalue$ be the largest positive real number such that
$inclusive(constantvalue) - minimalvalue constantvalue \geq 0$ on $[0,1]$; then
\begin{align*}
\int_{-1}^1 |nonfunction(constantvalue)|\,dconstantvalue &= \int_{-1}^1 |irrelevant(constantvalue)inclusive(constantvalue)|\,dconstantvalue \\
&> \int_{-1}^1 |irrelevant(constantvalue)(inclusive(constantvalue)-minimalvalue constantvalue)|\,dconstantvalue,
\end{align*}
and $irrelevant(constantvalue)(inclusive(constantvalue)-minimalvalue constantvalue)$ has more roots in $[0,1]$ than does $nonfunction$
(and has the same value at 0).
Repeating this argument shows that $\int_{0}^1 |nonfunction(constantvalue)|\,dconstantvalue$
is greater than the corresponding integral for some polynomial
with all of its roots in $[0,1]$.

Under this assumption, we have
\[
nonfunction(constantvalue) = variablecoef \prod_{i=1}^{1999} (constantvalue-peakvalue)
\]
for some $peakvalue \in (0,1]$. Since
\[
nonfunction(0) = -variablecoef \prod peakvalue = 1,
\]
we have
\[
|variablecoef| \geq \prod |peakvalue^{-1}| \geq 1.
\]

Thus it suffices to prove that if $irrelevant(constantvalue)$ is a \emph{monic} polynomial
of degree 1999 with all of its roots in $[0,1]$,
then $\int_0^1 |irrelevant(constantvalue)|\,dconstantvalue \geq smallvalue$ for some constant $smallvalue>0$. But the
integral of $\int_0^1 \prod_{i=1}^{1999} |constantvalue-peakvalue|\,dconstantvalue$ is a continuous
function for $peakvalue \in [0,1]$. The product of all of these intervals is
compact, so the integral achieves a minimum value for some $peakvalue$. This
minimum is the desired $smallvalue$.

Third solution (by Abe Kunin):
It suffices to prove the stronger inequality
\[
\sup_{constantvalue \in [-1,1]} |nonfunction(constantvalue)| \leq unbounded \int_{-1}^1 |nonfunction(constantvalue)|\,dconstantvalue
\]
holds for some $unbounded$.
But this follows immediately from the following standard fact: any two
norms on a finite-dimensional vector space (here the polynomials of
degree at most 1999) are equivalent. (The proof of this statement is also
a compactness argument: $unbounded$ can be taken to be the maximum of the
L1-norm divided by the sup norm over the set of polynomials with
L1-norm 1.)

Note: combining the first two approaches gives a constructive solution with
a constant that is better than that given by the first solution,
but is still far from optimal. I don't know
offhand whether it is even known what the optimal constant and/or the
polynomials achieving that constant are.

garbled_string.question:
Prove that there is a constant $vbnkqzfs$ such that, if $zgqxvndh(mcltzrga)$ is a polynomial
of degree 1999, then
\[|zgqxvndh(0)|\leq vbnkqzfs \int_{-1}^1 |zgqxvndh(mcltzrga)|\,d mcltzrga.\]
garbled_string.solution:
First solution: (by Reid Barton)
Let $r_1, \dots, r_{1999}$ be the roots of $rqwxlypdf$. Draw a disc of radius
$\mnlwczva$ around each $ypmvkeja$, where $\mnlwczva < 1/3998$; this
disc covers a subinterval of $[-1/2,1/2]$
of length at most $2\mnlwczva$, and so of the 2000 (or fewer) uncovered
intervals in $[-1/2,1/2]$,
one, which we call $I$, has length at least $\qvzxpokr = (1-3998\mnlwczva)/2000
> 0$.
We will exhibit an explicit lower bound for the integral of $|rqwxlypdf(mcltzrga)|/rqwxlypdf(0)$ over this
interval, which will yield such a bound for the entire integral.

Note that
\[
\frac{|rqwxlypdf(mcltzrga)|}{|rqwxlypdf(0)|} = \prod_{i=1}^{1999} \frac{|mcltzrga-ypmvkeja|}{|ypmvkeja|}.
\]
Also note that by construction, $|mcltzrga - ypmvkeja| \geq \mnlwczva$ for each $mcltzrga \in I$.
If $|ypmvkeja| \leq 1$, then we have $\frac{|mcltzrga-ypmvkeja|}{|ypmvkeja|} \geq \mnlwczva$. If $|ypmvkeja| > 1$, then
\[
\frac{|mcltzrga-ypmvkeja|}{|ypmvkeja|} = |1 - mcltzrga/ypmvkeja| \geq 1 - |mcltzrga/ypmvkeja| \geq 1/2
> \mnlwczva.
\]
We conclude that $\int_I |rqwxlypdf(mcltzrga)/rqwxlypdf(0)|\,d mcltzrga \geq \qvzxpokr \mnlwczva$,
independent of $rqwxlypdf$.

Second solution:
It will be a bit more convenient to assume $rqwxlypdf(0) = 1$ (which we
may achieve by rescaling unless $rqwxlypdf(0)=0$, in which case
there is nothing to prove) and to prove that there exists $qlwmsteh>0$
such that $\int_{-1}^1 |rqwxlypdf(mcltzrga)|\,d mcltzrga \geq qlwmsteh$, or even
such that $\int_0^1 |rqwxlypdf(mcltzrga)|\,d mcltzrga \geq qlwmsteh$.

We first reduce to the case where $rqwxlypdf$ has all of its roots in
$[0,1]$. If this is not the case, we can
factor $rqwxlypdf(mcltzrga)$ as $kjsnmbtq(mcltzrga) flhdpzsv(mcltzrga)$, where $kjsnmbtq$ has all roots in
the interval and $flhdpzsv$ has none. Then $flhdpzsv$ is either always
positive or always negative on $[0,1]$; assume the former.
Let $dpqvrmxe$ be the largest positive real number such that
$flhdpzsv(mcltzrga) - dpqvrmxe mcltzrga \geq 0$ on $[0,1]$; then
\begin{align*}
\int_{-1}^1 |rqwxlypdf(mcltzrga)|\,d mcltzrga &= \int_{-1}^1 |kjsnmbtq(mcltzrga)flhdpzsv(mcltzrga)|\,d mcltzrga \\
&> \int_{-1}^1 |kjsnmbtq(mcltzrga)(flhdpzsv(mcltzrga)-dpqvrmxe mcltzrga)|\,d mcltzrga,
\end{align*}
and $kjsnmbtq(mcltzrga)(flhdpzsv(mcltzrga)-dpqvrmxe mcltzrga)$ has more roots in $[0,1]$ than does $rqwxlypdf$
(and has the same value at 0).
Repeating this argument shows that $\int_{0}^1 |rqwxlypdf(mcltzrga)|\,d mcltzrga$
is greater than the corresponding integral for some polynomial
with all of its roots in $[0,1]$.

Under this assumption, we have
\[
rqwxlypdf(mcltzrga) = hqtsrlyn \prod_{i=1}^{1999} (mcltzrga-ypmvkeja)
\]
for some $ypmvkeja \in (0,1]$. Since
\[
rqwxlypdf(0) = -hqtsrlyn \prod ypmvkeja = 1,
\]
we have
\[
|hqtsrlyn| \geq \prod |ypmvkeja^{-1}| \geq 1.
\]

Thus it suffices to prove that if $kjsnmbtq(mcltzrga)$ is a \emph{monic} polynomial
of degree 1999 with all of its roots in $[0,1]$,
then $\int_0^1 |kjsnmbtq(mcltzrga)|\,d mcltzrga \geq qlwmsteh$ for some constant $qlwmsteh>0$. But the
integral $\int_0^1 \prod_{i=1}^{1999} |mcltzrga-ypmvkeja|\,d mcltzrga$ is a continuous
function for $ypmvkeja \in [0,1]$. The product of all of these intervals is
compact, so the integral achieves a minimum value for some $ypmvkeja$. This
minimum is the desired $qlwmsteh$.

Third solution (by Abe Kunin):
It suffices to prove the stronger inequality
\[
\sup_{mcltzrga \in [-1,1]} |rqwxlypdf(mcltzrga)| \leq vbnkqzfs \int_{-1}^1 |rqwxlypdf(mcltzrga)|\,d mcltzrga
\]
holds for some $vbnkqzfs$.
But this follows immediately from the following standard fact: any two
norms on a finite-dimensional vector space (here the polynomials of
degree at most 1999) are equivalent. (The proof of this statement is also
a compactness argument: $vbnkqzfs$ can be taken to be the maximum of the
L1-norm divided by the sup norm over the set of polynomials with
L1-norm 1.)

Note: combining the first two approaches gives a constructive solution with
a constant that is better than that given by the first solution,
but is still far from optimal. I don't know
offhand whether it is even known what the optimal constant and/or the
polynomials achieving that constant are.

kernel_variant.question:
Let n = 2024 and fix a non-negative integer k = 50.  Show that there exists an absolute constant C (depending only on n and k, but independent of the particular polynomial) with the following property:

For every real polynomial P of degree at most n one has  
  max_{0 \leq  j \leq  k} |P^{(j)}(2)|  \leq   C \cdot  \int _1^3 |P(x)| dx.                                        (\star )

In words: not only the value of P at the interior point 2 but the first fifty derivatives at that point are all controlled by the single L^1-norm of P on the interval [1, 3].

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
(\approx  560 words)

We break the argument into four transparent steps, each mirroring the logical layering of the reference solution while adding a derivative component.

Step 1.  Two auxiliary norms on a finite-dimensional space.  
Let V := {real polynomials of degree \leq  n}.  This is a real vector space of dimension N = n+1.  On V introduce

 \|P\|_1 := \int _1^3 |P(x)| dx,                (1)

 \|P\|\infty  := sup_{x\in [1,3]} |P(x)|,            (2)

 \|P\|_(k_) := max_{0\leq j\leq k} |P^{(j)}(2)|.          (3)

The quantity (3) is the norm that appears on the left-hand side of (\star ).  Observe that (1) and (2) were already present in the easier kernel, whereas (3) is new.

Step 2.  Equivalence of norms implies a crude bound.  
Because V is finite-dimensional, any two norms on V are equivalent.  In particular, there is a constant A=A(n,k)>0 such that  
 \|P\|_(k_) \leq  A \|P\|\infty  for all P\in V.                                           (4)

(Indeed, take the unit sphere S = {P : \|P\|\infty  = 1}; the mapping P \mapsto  \|P\|_(k_) is continuous on S and S is compact, so the maximum value of that map is finite; call it A.)

Step 3.  A Markov-type inequality converting derivatives into sup-norm.  
Classical Markov inequalities on a closed interval J of length 2 give, for every 0\leq j\leq k and every polynomial of degree \leq  n,

  |P^{(j)}(x)| \leq  (n)^{j} \cdot  2^{-j} \cdot  (n+1 choose j) \|P\|\infty   for all x\in J.    (5)

Specialising (5) to x=2 and taking maxima over j\leq k we get

 \|P\|_(k_) \leq  M(n,k) \|P\|\infty ,                                                 (6)

where an explicit choice is M(n,k) = max_{0\leq j\leq k} (n)^j 2^{-j}(n+1 choose j).  Compare (4): (6) is still of the form ``derivatives \leq  const\cdot sup'', but with a concrete constant whose growth we completely control; this will matter later when estimating how the overall C depends on n and k.

Step 4.  Transfer from sup-norm to L^1-norm.  
Return to the L^1-norm introduced in (1).  As in the reference solution, equivalence of norms on V yields a constant B=B(n)>0 satisfying

 \|P\|\infty  \leq  B \|P\|_1  for all P\in V.                                           (7)

Combine (6) and (7):

 \|P\|_(k_) \leq  M(n,k) \|P\|\infty  \leq  M(n,k) B \|P\|_1.                         (8)

Define  

 C := M(n,k) B,                                                         (9)

a constant depending solely on the fixed integers n and k.  Inequality (8) is exactly statement (\star ), completing the proof.

Remark 1.  Compactness was employed twice: first to bound evaluation of derivatives by the sup-norm (Step 2), second to relate the sup- and L^1-norms (Step 4).  The new ingredient vis-a-vis the original kernel is Step 3, which shows how classical approximation theory blends with linear-algebraic norm equivalence to control an entire jet (value and fifty derivatives) at a single point.

Remark 2.  Nothing essential changes if we replace the point x=2 by any interior point of [1,3] or adjust the interval endpoints: only the constants in (5) and hence in (9) would alter slightly.
